title: Iraq Bypasses Hormuz With Syria Pipeline Deal, Pirate Seizures Spread in Gulf of Aden, Magnitude 7.3 Quake Hits Mexico
subhead: Baghdad signs a landmark deal to route oil through Syria's Mediterranean coast, Somali pirates hijack a second tanker in three months, and a powerful earthquake strikes off southern Mexico — as the US-Iran war enters its seventh night and Ukraine reels from the sacking of its defence minister.

sections.heading: Iraq and Syria Revive Kirkuk-to-Mediterranean Pipeline
sections.body:
Iraq signed preliminary agreements with Western oil companies at a US-Iraq business summit in Washington on Friday, including a deal with Chevron to rehabilitate the long-defunct Kirkuk-to-Baniyas crude oil pipeline running from northern Iraq to Syria's Mediterranean coast. The US State Department called it 'a critical energy corridor linking Iraqi oil production to Mediterranean export markets and beyond', with an initial capacity of 2 million barrels per day. US ambassador to Turkiye Tom Barrack said the programme would 'make the Strait of Hormuz an afterthought.'

The deal, signed as the US-Iran war chokes off Hormuz traffic, represents a structural shift: Iraq — OPEC's second-largest producer — is actively building export routes that bypass the Persian Gulf entirely. The agreements also include two additional Chevron deals to boost Iraqi production and a formal agreement for Starlink to operate in Iraq. Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi said Baghdad had an 'open-door policy' for foreign investment. Total initial deals span energy, healthcare and technology, worth more than $60bn according to Reuters.

The pipeline's route through Syria, a country still under sanctions and partially controlled by various armed groups, raises questions about security and political feasibility. But the symbolic weight is clear: Middle Eastern energy is being rerouted away from Iranian-controlled chokepoints, and the US is actively facilitating it.
sections.whyItMatters: If the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline becomes operational, it would create one of the few major overland oil corridors out of the Persian Gulf, reducing dependence on Hormuz and reshaping regional energy logistics — provided Syria's fractured territory can support it.

sections.heading: US-Iran War: Seventh Night of Strikes, Escalation Warnings
sections.body:
The US military launched its seventh consecutive night of strikes on Iran on Friday. CENTCOM said the operations were 'designed to continue degrading Iranian military capabilities at the Commander in Chief's direction.' Explosions were reported in Yazd in central Iran, at Qeshm island, Bandar Abbas, and across several southern provinces, according to Iranian state media.

The scope of strikes has expanded. Satellite imagery analysed by Al Jazeera's open-source unit shows impact scars inside the perimeter of Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant complex between July 7 and July 12 — the first confirmed damage to the area surrounding Iran's only operational nuclear reactor. A Bushehr provincial official denied the reactor itself was hit, and the IAEA has recorded multiple projectile incidents at the site in 2026. The US has not identified Bushehr or any nuclear facility as a target.

Meanwhile, Iran's Revolutionary Guards claimed to have struck a US drone depot in Bahrain and attacked US military facilities in Kuwait, Jordan, and Syria. Kuwait confirmed Iranian drone strikes damaged a power plant and water desalination facility, injuring soldiers and prompting citizens to ration electricity. CBS reported that several American service members were injured in Iranian attacks on two Jordanian bases over the past week. Major-General Mohsen Rezaei, an adviser to Iran's supreme leader, warned that if US strikes continue for another two to three days, Iran would shift from deterrence to 'offensive operations' and 'no political border will be safe.'
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict is now in a dangerous escalatory cycle. Strikes are reaching closer to nuclear infrastructure, Iran is hitting Gulf state utilities, and both sides are raising the stakes beyond the original Hormuz dispute. The Bushehr damage — even if incidental — introduces nuclear safety risks that could draw international intervention.

sections.heading: Ukraine Soldiers Outraged as Zelensky Ousts Defence Minister Fedorov
sections.body:
President Zelensky's decision not to re-appoint Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov in a cabinet reshuffle has sparked outrage among Ukraine's soldiers and anti-corruption activists. BBC interviews with troops — many speaking anonymously due to army orders against political commentary — described the move as 'a slap in the face' to service members. Fedorov, 35, was widely credited with driving military innovation: expanding drone warfare, securing Elon Musk's cooperation to block Russian Starlink access, and advancing AI-powered interceptors to protect cities from Russian drones.

The rupture appears rooted in a feud between Fedorov and General Oleksandr Syrskyi, the 60-year-old commander-in-chief who soldiers nicknamed 'General 200' (a reference to the Soviet military code for casualties) and 'The Butcher.' Zelensky said the two men could not even be in the same room together; Fedorov blamed Syrskyi for blocking his reforms. Analyst Ivan Stupak described the divide as 'an iPhone 16 versus a telephone from the 1980s.' The acting replacement is Yevhenii Khmara, head of the Security Service of Ukraine.

Anti-corruption campaigner Daria Kaleniuk warned that Fedorov's removal could halt his reform programme at a critical moment, with winter approaching and Russia likely to target Ukraine's energy grid again. Putin's approval rating has reportedly fallen to 65-66%, its lowest since the full-scale invasion began, according to Russian pollsters FOM and VTsIOM. Russia also cracked down on domestic dissent on Friday, fining politician Boris Nadezhdin and jailing blogger Ilya Remeslo for anti-war posts.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has ousted its most effective defence moderniser at a time when its drone and tech innovations were helping turn the tide. The move exposes a generational fault line in Ukraine's military leadership and risks stalling momentum ahead of a difficult winter campaign.

sections.heading: Somali Pirates Seize Second Tanker as Gulf of Aden Suffers Security Vacuum
sections.body:
A tanker, the MT Asana, was hijacked in the Gulf of Aden off the coast of Yemen on Friday — the second vessel seized by suspected Somali pirates in three months. Puntland security officials said seven gunmen boarded the Tanzania-flagged tanker 65 nautical miles south of Mukalla as it sailed toward Bosaso. The French navy's Mica Centre has recorded 18 piracy incidents and hijackings since April, with at least three vessels still held for ransom.

The resurgence comes after more than a decade of dormancy. Between 2005 and 2012, Somali pirates carried out more than 1,000 attacks and collected roughly $400m in ransoms, prompting a multinational naval crackdown that largely ended hijackings by 2013. The shipping industry lifted its 'high risk' designation for the Indian Ocean in 2023. Analysts point to three factors: naval forces stretched thin by the Red Sea and Hormuz conflicts, shipping traffic rerouted closer to Somali waters, and instability within Somalia itself. The Gulf of Aden is more lightly patrolled than the Indian Ocean, where the EU's Atalanta mission operates.

On Thursday, EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas signed a Status of Forces Agreement with Djibouti to secure continued access for the Atalanta and Aspides naval missions, which have protected more than 670 merchant vessels in two and a half years. Egypt is working to free sailors held on a tanker seized in May.
sections.whyItMatters: Piracy is exploiting the security vacuum created by two concurrent naval conflicts. As war draws patrol ships to Hormuz and the Red Sea, Somali pirates are returning to one of the world's busiest shipping lanes — compounding the disruption to global maritime trade.

sections.heading: Trump Alleges China Meddled in 2020 Election, Threatens Canada With New Tariffs Over Wildfire Smoke
sections.body:
Donald Trump delivered a primetime White House address on Thursday in which he accused China of interfering in the 2020 election and alleged 'shocking vulnerabilities' in US voting systems ahead of November's midterms. He claimed China illicitly acquired 220 million voter files and declassified hundreds of intelligence documents, though many were heavily redacted and no evidence was presented that China altered voting systems or outcomes. The US intelligence community's own 2021 assessment concluded with 'high confidence' that China did not interfere in 2020. China's foreign ministry called the allegations 'entirely fabricated.' Democrats accused Trump of undermining confidence in the midterms; former Vice-President Kamala Harris said he wanted voters to 'lose confidence in our electoral system so you stay home this November.'

Separately, Trump threatened new tariffs on Canada over 'filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air' from Ontario's wildfires, which have burned nearly 3 million hectares and pushed air quality in Detroit to worst in the world. Ontario Premier Doug Ford responded by telling US lawmakers to 'send help' rather than complain, noting that Canadian crews are battling 838 active fires. Canada has invested roughly $12bn in forest sustainability and fire prevention. The World Cup final between Argentina and Spain at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey on Sunday may be affected by haze, with White House officials holding informal meetings with FIFA to monitor conditions.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump is weaponising two distinct domestic anxieties — election integrity and environmental health — for political positioning ahead of the midterms. The Canada tariff threat over wildfire smoke has no clear trade mechanism, but signals that no bilateral relationship is insulated from his domestic agenda.

sections.heading: China Condemns British Steel Nationalisation, EU Relaxes Carbon Cuts, Magnitude 7.3 Quake Hits Mexico
sections.body:
China's commerce ministry said it 'firmly opposes and is strongly dissatisfied' with the UK's nationalisation of British Steel, accusing Britain of 'forcibly' taking control of the Scunthorpe plant from China's Jingye Group 'in the name of national security' and calling it a serious infringement of Chinese firms' rights. The UK government said negotiations with Jingye failed to reach a deal representing value to the taxpayer. The plant, which directly employs 2,700 people and is costing the government over £1m per day to run, produces steel types not made anywhere else in Britain. The dispute arrives just days before incoming prime minister Andy Burnham enters Downing Street on Monday, forcing him to balance national security concerns against the economic benefits of ties with China.

The European Commission proposed slowing the rate at which businesses must cut emissions under the EU's emissions trading system, reducing the annual cap reduction from 4.3% to roughly 3.7% from 2031 and extending free permits from 2034 to 2038. Climate commissioner Wopke Hoekstra called it a 'more business-friendly' approach; Green MEPs warned it would produce 'gigantic climate pollution.' Poland said it would push for further weakening.

A magnitude 7.3 earthquake struck off the coast of southern Mexico near Puerto Madero on Friday at a depth of 15.2km, triggering a tsunami warning for parts of the Pacific coast. Waves of 0.3m were recorded in Mexico; no fatalities or significant damage were reported. Aftershocks of magnitude 4.7 to 6.0 followed. The tsunami warning was later lifted. Guatemala and El Salvador reported evacuations.
sections.whyItMatters: Three stories with a common thread: the tension between national security and globalisation. Britain chose strategic autonomy over Chinese investment; Europe chose industrial competitiveness over climate ambition; and Mexico got a reminder of its seismic vulnerability on the Pacific rim.

bottomLine: The US-Iran war entered its seventh night with strikes creeping closer to nuclear infrastructure and Iran threatening full-scale offensive operations if bombing continues — but the day's most consequential long-term shift may be Iraq's deal to route 2 million barrels per day through a Syria-to-Mediterranean pipeline, bypassing Hormuz entirely. Meanwhile, Somalia's pirates are filling the security vacuum left by diverted naval forces, Ukraine sacked the minister who modernised its military, and Trump used a primetime address to cast doubt on the midterms while threatening Canada with tariffs over wildfire smoke. The global order is fracturing along multiple seams at once.
